title: We graded every team’s 2026 Giro d’Italia
content:
Cyclist
We graded every team’s 2026 Giro d’Italia

After three weeks of racing around Bulgaria, Italy and Switzerland, the Giro d’Italia 2026 reached its conclusion last night in Rome.

In front of the Colosseum, it was Jonas Vingegaard who lifted the Trofeo Senza Fine as Giro d’Italia champion, having won five stages during his grand tour of Italy. The Dane’s comprehensive victory saw him pull out a five-minute lead over second place finisher Felix Gall, however the GC results are not the only thing that defines a team’s performance at a Grand Tour. Plenty of squads came into this race with ambitions of sprint glory, breakaway antics or simply a chance of getting on TV.

Out of the 23 teams that started the Giro d’Italia, nine claimed stage wins, 11 teams were represented inside the top 20 of the overall rankings and seven ended up winning special prizes in Rome.

As the dust begins to settle on the 109th Giro d’Italia, we’ve put together a report card for every team. To settle on our scores from A to F, we’ll factor in roster strength, pre-race ambitions, results and visibility throughout the race.

Alpecin-Premier Tech: D-

I’ll admit, Alpecin-Premier Tech is a pretty terrible place to start.

It’s tough to adapt in a Grand Tour when your team leader crashes out on day one. Indeed, Kaden Groves’s tumble left Alpecin-Premier Tech without a sprinter to lead out, leaving them to pick up scraps in the few sprints that transpired at this year’s Giro.

They were in a couple of breakaways here and there, but there’s ultimately nothing of note for Alpecin at this race.

Bahrain Victorious: A

Bahrain Victorious have had a couple of turbulent Giro performances in recent years. However, this year the squad bounced back to their best, finishing the race with two riders inside the top ten, a week in pink and a stage victory.

Afonso Eulálio served up a Giro performance that will long live in the memory of fans. Apart from that week in pink, the Portuguese rider was aggressive throughout this race and was rewarded with a hard-fought sixth place overall and the maglia bianca. I reckon Eulálio will go down as the star of this Giro d’Italia.

As for the remainder of the team, Alec Segaert claimed an impressive win on Stage 12, Damiano Caruso managed to seal a top ten overall in his final Giro, and Edoardo Zambanini brushed shoulders with Paul Magnier to come second on Stage 18.

Bardiani CSF: C+

No Bardiani, no Giro.

This Italian team did what they do best: get in the breakaway and hope for the best. With a roster of riders mostly under the age of 25, Bardiani scored five top ten finishes in total, which isn’t too bad for a relatively weak team on paper. Manuele Tarozzi won the intermediate sprint prize too, which offers the team a little bit of pocket money at least.

For a team working on a budget of a Curly Wurly and a dream, they managed to feature on TV far more than some of their WorldTour contemporaries.

Decathlon CMA CGM: A

Decathlon CMA CGM are amid a renaissance. Yes, Paul Seixas brings a big part of that, but the French team’s Giro d’Italia proves that they can overachieve even without the teenage sensation on their roster.

The headline here is Felix Gall, who managed to pick up the silver medal in GC. The Austrian, who finished in fifth at the Tour last year, was consistently the best of the rest behind Vingegaard in the high mountains. That was the case throughout this Giro, from the first mountaintop finish to the last. To their credit, the team rallied around their leader to secure a convincing second place finish, which was somewhat unexpected at the beginning of the race. Major props for this have to be handed to Gregor Muhlberger and Callum Scotson, who excelled in the mountains and hung on much longer than some of the big-name domestiques on rival teams.

Tobias Lund Andresen also managed to score several top ten finishes in the few sprint opportunities – while also battling through illness – and the team walk away from this Giro with one of the highest prize money pools.

Given that this was their second-tier roster, they performed well above expectations.

EF Education EasyPost: B

Richard Carapaz decided to skip the Giro at the last minute, so EF Education-EasyPost had to go for their back-up plan. Luckily, that role as a breakaway and stage-hunting force has often paid dividends for the team, and it did here once again.

The team took their obligatory Giro stage win courtesy of Michael Valgren, but the roster shone as a collective in the breakaways and, in the case of numerous top ten finisher Madis Mihkels, in the sprints. I expected a little more from Ireland’s Darren Rafferty, who had been experimenting in the breaks in the build-up to this Giro, but Markel Beloki delivered on his promise, as did serial escapee and mountains classification contender Jardi van der Lee.

On the whole, it was exactly what you’d expect from EF. Easy B grade.

Groupama-FDJ United: F

Is it criminal for Groupama-FDJ United to care about the Giro d’Italia? For the past few years, it’s felt that way, given their continuous lack of results in the race.

Josh Kench was the team’s only standout rider in my eyes, although Rémi Cavagna at least gambled in the odd breakaway and had a good TT. Apart from that, I genuinely questioned whether this team had pulled a mutiny and quit quietly. That was until Paul Penhoët’s surprise podium finish on the final day in Rome.

I don’t think I need to spend more of my time reflecting on this team.

Jayco-AlUla: F

The gloves are off. Jayco-AlUla came into this Giro d’Italia with hopes of hunting stages and maybe bagging a top ten finish overall. They achieved nothing of the sort.

Jayco-AlUla brought a relatively strong lineup, but the team were blunt as anything at this Giro d’Italia, with just four top ten finishes and one of the smallest UCI points hauls from any team at the race.

Most of the credit for that has to be handed to Ben O’Connor, who rode an uninspired GC campaign to finish in 16th place overall. Yes, he had an excellent time-trial, but his climbing performances were well below what’s expected for a top ten contender. At this point, I don’t understand how a team can confidently call O’Connor their GC leader when his last top ten in a stage race was over a year ago (and that’s not for the lack of trying).

Beyond O’Connor’s collapse in the GC, the team were nowhere to be seen. In fairness, they were hit quite hard by crashes and illness. That forced out two-time Giro stage winner Andrea Vendrame as well as the team’s new sprinter Pascal Ackermann. However, apart from Bob Donaldson being victim of a controversial headbutt on Stage 15, I can’t recall a memorable Jayco-AlUla moment from this Giro.

Lidl-Trek: B+

After three weeks of near misses, Lidl-Trek managed to get themselves together for the final week. Jonathan Milan won a stage, Giulio Ciccone secured the mountains jersey and Derek Gee-West came good to secure a top five finish. Don’t forget that day in the maglia rosa for Ciccone either.

The team ticked most boxes, apart from the points classification, which Milan failed to win for the first time. For that reason, among others, I can’t help but feel disappointed by Milan’s Giro. Up until Rome, he was serving up miss after miss in the sprints, while his leadout train looked out of control fairly often on flat stages. It’s the weakest I’ve seen him since 2023.

Ciccone, being the enigma that he is, was plucky enough to take pink early on, and smart enough to win the maglia azzurra in the space of two stages. He was cunning on the whole, but I feel the Italian lost some fans during this Giro for his head-scratching tactics, especially when it came to supporting – or not supporting – Gee-West.

Gee-West rode yet another steady Grand Tour himself. He lost some big time in week one, but managed to claw enough back to reach fifth, which felt very deserved after a strong final week in the mountains.

To be honest, this is quite similar to what we would have forecasted for Lidl-Trek three weeks ago. That shouldn’t diminish a strong race, though.

Lotto-Intermarché: D-

It’s been four years since either Lotto or Intermarché last won a Giro stage. Now combined, they’ve managed to continue that drought for another year, albeit with half a lineup by the time the race concluded in Rome.

Lennart Van Eetvelt looked strong in the race’s first week, but faded away when the high mountains arrived. A broken finger forced him out of the race, but that doesn’t excuse the remainder of the team’s relative inactivity. In the end, experienced cyclocross specialist Toon Aerts would be the closest Lotto would come to a stage victory, finishing second on the day won by Alec Segaert.

On the whole though, this team didn’t really light anyone’s fire.

Movistar: C+

Movistar are another team with quite the Grand Tour drought. The Spanish team haven’t claimed a Grand Tour stage since May 2024. Sadly, that continues, but it’s not without trying at this Giro.

The team delivered some really good individual performances. Einer Rubio was a fascinating character to watch in the breakaways, Orlius Aular was close to a win on Stage 4, and Lorenzo Milesi was brilliant throughout this race, even when Enric Mas tumbled down the overall standings.

On the whole, I think the team is a little hard done by not having a stage win from this Giro. However, they ultimately leave empty-handed and without a stage win or top ten to write home about.

Netcompany Ineos: B

The 2026 Giro was Netcompany Ineos’s first Grand Tour with their new name and AI tech sponsor.

The team’s first-choice leader Egan Bernal needed more than AI to keep him in the clear though. The Colombian was disappointing for a lot of this race, but he had his moments during the final week. At least Bernal’s downfall made way for Thymen Arensman to enjoy his best Grand Tour to date. That wouldn’t be enough to move him onto the podium though, despite his fantastic time-trial. Perhaps that’s a damning assessment of Arensman’s potential, perhaps it’s a sign of new-found consistency he can build on.

Nevertheless, the team leave with a stage win, courtesy of Pippo Ganna’s domination in the time-trial, as well as two riders inside the GC top ten. For a team of this pedigree and budget, these results are exactly what you’d predict.

NSN Cycling: D-

NSN’s Giro d’Italia was bang average.

They managed to pick up a healthy amount of top ten finishes with a varied pool of riders, and Jan Hirt managed to ride to 12th overall, but that’s to be expected from this Giro d’Italia expert. Ethan Vernon was also up there in a couple of sprints as well, but this team were more visible than influential.

Alessandro Pinarello looked to be in great form ahead of the race, but ultimately raced an underwhelming Giro. That aside, I don’t think anyone really expected much more of this team.

Picnic-PostNL: F

I forgive you for asking yourself whether Picnic-PostNL were even here, but let me give you the gist. This team were dull as dishwater at the Giro.

The roster looked decent on paper, with names like Warren Barguil, Frank van den Broek and sprinter Casper van Uden wheeled out for the Corsa Rosa. In reality, the team would fall right on its face, scoring just two top ten finishes on stages and finishing with the smallest amount of both UCI points and prize money.

Barguil’s efforts in the breakaway had all the spice of a plain bowl of rice, while breakaway wildcard Van den Broek failed to even reach the top 80 on the results sheet. That one has absolutely dumbfounded me, given his strong results in previous Grand Tours.

This team continues to lower the bar of my expectations. It’s almost on the floor now. For the love of God, improve by the Tour de France.

Pinarello-Q36.5: C+

Pinarello-Q36.5 were the Giro’s shiny new toy last year, but their sophomore Giro was met with a lot less hype. That aside, the results are wholly acceptable, with a handful of top tens and two riders in the top 15 places in GC.

Sprinter Matteo Moschetti was ruled out early, but they still picked up some solid results elsewhere, mainly in the race’s only time-trial, in which they had three riders inside the top 15. The team managed to score some surprise GC results, albeit in the minor placings. Chris Harper was the best placed, but credit has to be given to veteran David de la Cruz as well for finishing inside the top 20 overall.

The team haven’t managed to pick up that all-important stage win, but their first Pidcock-less Grand Tour proved fruitful enough.

Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe: B

Much like last year, Plan A didn’t quite go as imagined for Red Bull. Giulio Pellizzari looked strong early on, but illness and patches of bad form led him to drop almost half an hour in the GC on one stage.

Like last year, Plan B, in this case Jai Hindley, went ahead accordingly. The Australian was consistently the third strongest climber at this Giro, and the team supported him well during the final week. Ultimately, this paid dividends with a third-place finish overall, which went a long way in salvaging something out of this Giro.

Sasha Vlasov was also good in the breakaways, but there’s no stage win to discuss here. Given how strong the roster was at the start of this race, that’s somewhat disappointing.

Soudal Quick-Step: A

We got some classic Quick-Step at the Giro d’Italia. The Belgian team returned to its former glory by claiming the points classification, the team’s first since Mark Cavendish’s 2021 Tour green jersey, as well as three stages from their French sprinter Paul Magnier.

While Magnier carried this team to victory, Jasper Stuyven was also in good shape during this Giro, culminating in three top five finishes. He was the best leadout man of the race, putting even Simone Consonni to shame.

Away from the sprinters, Quick-Step’s Italians were strong in the breakaway. Gianmarco Garofoli and Andrea Raccagni impressed, with the latter making a big breakthrough at this Grand Tour.

At long last, it seems as though Soudal Quick-Step are back to their best, at least on a traditional front. After years of playing it safe in a GC realm, Magnier has brought back some flair to this Grand Tour stalwart.

Polti-VisitMalta: B

Polti-VisitMalta have been the most successful of the Italian wildcard teams in recent years.

This year, the team were perhaps the most visible of the Italian wildcards too. They were up in the breakaway almost every day, resulting in a fair pool of prize money from those intermediate prizes. Early KOM leader Diego Sevilla won the Fuga prize handed to the rider who spends the most time at the front of the race.

The team made the top ten on ten occasions throughout this race, including two podium spots. The most notable of which was the heartbreak of Mirco Maestri’s Stage 15 defeat to Frederik Dversnes. I’d like to highlight a top ten finisher in Ludovico Crescioli too, who really impressed me in the final week of his first Grand Tour.

Tudor: B+

This is Tudor’s third Giro d’Italia, but their best on a GC front. Indeed, the Swiss ProTeam scored their best-ever GC performance at a Grand Tour. That accolade was achieved by Michael Storer, who picked up a career-best seventh place overall after his steady GC campaign that saw him stuck in seventh from Stage 10 until the race’s conclusion.

Mathys Rondel was also a standout for the team in the GC. Finishing in 11th in Rome, the Frenchman overdelivered throughout the race and even spent a day in the white jersey, but lost time in the TT. The pair’s performances in the overall standings helped the squad achieve fourth place in the team classification, their best at a Grand Tour as well.

That may seem like scraps, but Tudor made regular appearances in the top ten on stages. With a total of 14 top ten finishes, Tudor were among the top performers, despite not picking up a stage win as proof.

Tudor can seem like a boring team sometimes, but I reckon their Giro was more exciting than usual.

UAE Team Emirates XRG: A

UAE Team Emirates-XRG are another team with a rather heavy asterisk on their name, following a heavy crash on Stage 2 that wiped out three of their key players.

Regardless, the team scooped up three stage wins from Jhonotan Narvaéz, plus another from Igor Arrieta, so it’s safe to say they did OK without Adam Yates and Jay Vine. Without their mountain leaders, the riders were given more liberty to chase their own successes and that paid off.

There was also that bizarre maglia ciclamino run for Narvaéz, who eventually quit the race two days early after an apparently clumsy crash, but that shouldn’t take away from what was a solid Giro performance all things considered.

Unibet Rose Rockets: B-

After getting rejected by the Tour de France, Unibet Rose Rockets used the Giro to make their Grand Tour debut.

Dylan Groenewegen was steady in the sprints, but missed out on the top step or indeed the runners-up spot on any stage. Wout Poels climbed well and Niklas Larsen managed to deliver an expected top ten in the time-trial. Unfortunately, the team’s best result could have been in Naples, when Elmar Reinders’ crash at the head of the race took Groenewegen out of the mix.

For a team that hadn’t ridden a Grand Tour before, they were visible and active throughout the race. That’s more than what can be said for the likes of Picnic-PostNL and Groupama-FDJ United.

Uno-X Mobility: A-

There was a lot of talk about Unibet as debutants, but don’t forget that this is Uno-X’s first time at the Giro d’Italia. You wouldn’t know it, given how consistent this team were throughout the three weeks.

The team managed to finish inside the top ten on 12 occasions, the sixth highest tally. Erlend Blikra, Martin Tjøtta, Johannes Kulset, Sakarias Løland, Markus Hoelgaard and Andreas Leknessund all contributed to that figure, with top tens in the sprint, breakaways and hilly days. That’s six of the eight-man roster, which shrinks to seven if you account for the early abandonment of Ådne Holter on Stage 2. The only rider in the team not on that list is Fredrik Dversnes, who didn’t just finish in the top ten, he won a stage.

You have to hand it to them. Uno-X animated the race. Andreas Leknessund was one of the breakaway characters of this Giro, finishing runner-up on three occasions from the escape group.

If they had a stronger climber, you bet they would have been up there even more often. Even without, Kulset managed to finish inside the top 20 overall. Not bad for a team of wildcards.

Visma-Lease a Bike: A+

What can you say about a team that wins the overall title and six stages? Well, excellence comes to mind.

Visma-Lease a Bike emerged as the strongest mountain team right from the first mountain stage, when Davide Piganzoli set up Vingegaard for the first of his five stage victories. Piganzoli, along with Sepp Kuss, Bart Lemmen and Tim Rex in particular, was a sensational teammate in the mountains for their Danish leader. Truly, a better team performance at this Giro, you’d be hard-pressed to find.

The only real miss is Piganzoli losing out on the white jersey, but that was only a concern really once the race progressed into the final week. He still finished eighth in GC, which is a good enough achievement for the Italian, who had never broken into the top ten before.

XDS Astana: A

XDS Astana came here without any top-drawer riders. What they achieved, on the other hand, was much better than anyone expected for this team, which has been overachieving for over 12 months now.

Guillermo Silva got the ball rolling in Bulgaria, with a stage win and pink jersey stint secured. The momentum would continue through the first week, with Davide Ballerini picking up a first Grand Tour stage, alongside countless top ten finishes from the likes of Christian Scaroni. Alberto Bettiol would make it a hat-trick of wins in week two, solidifying this team’s exceptional Giro.

Three stage wins, a maglia rosa stint and a whopping 15 top ten finishes are close to the ceiling for this team. What could have made this better is a top ten overall run for either Scaroni or Harold Martín López. Alas, they still achieved enough to warrant a good grade.

The post We graded every team’s 2026 Giro d’Italia appeared first on Cyclist.
author: Ewan Wilson | Staff Writer
